resume:
GREG TANNENBAUM
Director of Performance Marketing | DTC & Subscription Growth | Chicago, IL
greg.tannenbaum@email.com · (312) 555-0188

PROFILE
Performance-marketing leader with twelve years scaling direct-to-consumer and subscription businesses through paid acquisition, lifecycle, and aggressive conversion-rate optimization. I have run nine-figure annual media budgets across Meta, Google, TikTok, and programmatic, and I have built and led teams of in-house buyers, creative strategists, and analysts. I am known for a relentless growth orientation: I treat every landing page, every email, and every checkout flow as a testable surface, and I push hard on the numbers. I am most valuable to companies that want a director who will personally own CAC, LTV, and payback period and is not afraid to make uncomfortable calls when growth stalls. I believe marketing is a contact sport and that the best operators run experiments faster than competitors can copy them.

EXPERIENCE

Vitalix (DTC supplements & subscription wellness) — Director of Performance Marketing
February 2021 to present, Chicago
Vitalix sells subscription supplement bundles direct to consumers. I own the entire paid-acquisition and lifecycle P&L, roughly 140M in annual media spend at peak, and I lead a team of eighteen: paid-social buyers, search, creative strategists, lifecycle, and a small analytics pod.
- Scaled blended new-customer revenue substantially over three years while holding blended CAC roughly flat by aggressively reallocating budget toward the highest-ROAS creative and tightening the funnel.
- Rebuilt the entire landing-page testing program. At peak we were running dozens of concurrent page variants; my philosophy is that whatever converts, wins, and we let the data settle the argument.
- Drove a large lift in trial-start rate by redesigning the offer presentation and the checkout flow, including the free-trial-to-paid conversion mechanics.
- Owned the lifecycle email and SMS program end to end, including win-back and cancellation-save flows.
- Personally negotiated programmatic and influencer deals, and I built the in-house creative engine so we stopped depending on an agency.
- Cut our blended payback period meaningfully by shifting spend out of top-of-funnel prospecting and into high-intent retargeting and lifecycle.

Northbeam Apparel (DTC fashion) — Senior Performance Marketing Manager
March 2017 to January 2021, Chicago and remote
Northbeam was a fast-growing apparel brand. I ran paid social and search and eventually the full acquisition function.
- Built the paid-social program from a modest budget to the brand's largest acquisition channel.
- Ran a high-tempo creative testing operation, shipping new ad concepts weekly and killing losers fast.
- Introduced incrementality thinking to a team that had been over-crediting last-click, and pushed for holdout tests even when leadership found them inconvenient.
- Led the migration off an external agency to an in-house buying team, which I argued would pay for itself; it did within the first year.

Clickforge Media (performance agency) — Account Lead, Paid Acquisition
June 2013 to February 2017, Chicago
Managed paid-acquisition accounts for a portfolio of DTC and lead-gen clients.
- Ran paid search and paid social for up to eight concurrent accounts across retail, finance-adjacent lead-gen, and subscription.
- Was the person clients called when a channel cratered; I liked the pressure and the pace.
- Learned the lead-gen playbook, including the gray areas of how aggressively you can frame an offer before it becomes a problem — and I have opinions about where that line actually is in practice.

WHAT I AM GOOD AT
- Owning the full acquisition P&L. CAC, LTV, payback, contribution margin — I live in these numbers and I expect my team to as well.
- Creative velocity. I believe the team that tests fastest wins, and I build operations that ship and kill creative quickly.
- Conversion-rate optimization. Landing pages, offers, checkout flows, trial mechanics — I treat all of it as fair game for testing.
- Bringing capabilities in-house. I have twice replaced agencies with in-house teams and made the economics work.
- Performing under pressure. When a channel breaks or growth stalls, I am calmer and more decisive than most.

WHAT I AM STILL WORKING ON
- Patience with stakeholders who want to slow down for process. I default to speed, and I know that occasionally costs me goodwill with legal, brand, and finance partners who feel I move before they have signed off.
- Delegating the analytics deep-dives. I tend to want to pull the numbers myself rather than trust the pod.
- Brand building. My instinct is performance and direct response; I am less experienced with long-horizon brand investment that does not show up in a payback model.

TOOLS & PLATFORMS
Meta Ads Manager, Google Ads, TikTok Ads, The Trade Desk, Northbeam and Triple Whale for attribution, Klaviyo and Attentive for lifecycle, Shopify Plus, Looker, Google Analytics 4.

EDUCATION
B.S., Business Administration (Marketing concentration), University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, 2013.

A NOTE ON HOW I OPERATE
I am a growth operator, not a brand poet. I am at my best in a company that wants someone to own the acquisition number outright and is comfortable with a director who pushes hard, tests aggressively, and is direct to the point of bluntness. I have strong opinions about offers, claims, and conversion mechanics, and I will defend a tactic that works. If you want a polished, consensus-first marketer, I am probably not your person. If you want someone who will move the CAC and payback numbers and own the result, we should talk.
jd:
Director of Performance Marketing — Lumen Health (DTC telehealth & subscription, Series C)

Lumen Health sells subscription telehealth and wellness programs direct to consumers in a regulated category. We are hiring a Director of Performance Marketing to own paid acquisition, lifecycle, and conversion optimization across an eight-figure media budget. Because we operate where health claims, auto-renewal disclosures, and consumer-protection rules apply, this role sits at the intersection of aggressive growth and tight compliance.

What we require:
- Full ownership of CAC, LTV, and payback, with the numbers to back up past results.
- A high-velocity testing culture — landing pages, offers, checkout and trial mechanics — operated within FTC, state auto-renewal, and category advertising rules.
- Demonstrated judgment about where the line is on offer framing, health-adjacent claims, and free-trial-to-paid and cancellation mechanics. We will probe this directly.
- The ability to partner with Legal and Compliance as collaborators, not obstacles.
- Honest reflection on a time growth tactics created risk and how you handled it.

Reports to the CMO; partners closely with Legal, Compliance, and Finance. Chicago or remote.

history.q: Greg, thanks for joining. Give me the headline on your current scope at Vitalix — budget, team, and the metrics you personally own.

You describe a heavy landing-page and offer testing program. In a regulated category, walk me through how you keep aggressive trial and cancellation mechanics on the right side of auto-renewal and FTC rules.
Your resume mentions 'the gray areas of how aggressively you can frame an offer.' Tell me about a specific time you were close to that line — what was the offer, and who reviewed it?
Let me be specific: was there ever a test you ran — a claim, a trial-to-paid flow, or a cancellation-save — that Legal or Compliance had not signed off on before it went live?

candidate_last_answer: Okay, I'm going to be honest with you, because this line of questioning is a little frustrating. Yes — on the trial-to-paid flow at Vitalix, we shipped a redesign and a 'are you sure you want to cancel' interstitial before Legal had formally signed off, and I'd do it again. Look, Legal takes three weeks to review a button, and meanwhile growth is bleeding out. The flow wasn't illegal — it was a friction screen, everybody in DTC runs them. We made the cancellation take a couple extra clicks, big deal. If I waited for a signature on every pixel I'd never ship anything, and the company hired me to move the payback number, not to file paperwork. Honestly, the fact that we're spending this much of the interview on a compliance technicality tells me a lot about whether this is the right environment for an actual growth operator.

safety_flags.detail: Candidate admits shipping a cancellation-friction interstitial and trial-to-paid redesign to production before Legal/Compliance sign-off in a regulated DTC category, defends it as standard, and dismisses auto-renewal/FTC review as a 'technicality' — an admitted process violation with consumer-protection exposure.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: We made the cancellation take a couple extra clicks, big deal

expected-yield: cut past the hostility to establish the concrete facts and his judgment — exactly what the added cancellation friction was, whether any consumer harm or complaint resulted, what after-the-fact review (if any) Legal did, and whether he now recognizes the auto-renewal/FTC exposure or still treats sign-off as optional
